summary: O'Neil highlights two new examples of algorithmic surveillance systems that emerged after her book's publication. Joberate scores employees on their likelihood of job-hunting, essentially automating and scaling the behavior of a paranoid boss. Litify aims to use AI to predict litigation outcomes and transform legal finance. O'Neil presents both as troubling extensions of the 'weapons of math destruction' she warns about, noting the irony of companies claiming they're not creepy while engaging in surveillance capitalism.
key_points: Joberate creates 'J-scores' rating employees' flight risk using public data, automating workplace surveillance
Companies deflect criticism by claiming they only use 'publicly available data' as if that makes surveillance acceptable
Litigation finance is being transformed by algorithmic prediction, with unclear consequences for access to justice
The proliferation of scoring systems extends into ever more intimate aspects of work and legal life
money_quotes: Kind of like an overbearing, nosy boss, but in automated and scaled digital form.
They claim they're not creepy because they use only publicly available data.
key_insight: The automation of surveillance doesn't make it less invasive—it makes the overbearing boss omnipresent and inescapable.
